coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from bovine serum albumin and formic acid with one- and two-photon fluorescence for live cell nuclear imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Mingqian
Li, Xintong
Wu, Hao
Wang, Beibei
Wu, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with both one- and two-photon fluorescence have drawn great attention for biomedical imaging. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots were facilely developed by one-pot hydrothermal method using bovine serum albumin and formic acid as carbon sources. They are highly water-soluble with strong fluorescence when excited with ultraviolet or near infrared light. The carbon dots have a diameter of ∼8.32nm and can emit strong two-photon induced fluorescence upon excitation at 750nm with a femtosecond laser. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer analysis revealed that the carbon dots contained three components, C, N and O, corresponding to the peak at 285, 398 and 532eV, respectively. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analysis revealed that there are carboxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface, which allowed further linking of functional molecules. pH stability study demonstrated that the carbon dots are able to be used in a wide range of pH values. The fluorescence mechanism is also discussed in this study. Importantly, these carbon dots are biocompatible and highly photostable, which can be directly applied for both one- and two-photon living cell imaging. After proper surface functionalization with TAT peptide, they can be used as fluorescent probes for live cell nuclear-targeted imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Two-photon-induced fluorescence
Bioimaging
Nitrogen-doped
Nuclear-targeted imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image for C-dots prepared from BSA-FA. Inset shows the high resolution TEM image. (B) Particle size distribution of the C-dots. Inset shows the photograph of the C-dots irradiated with UV-light with water as control.
UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra for C-dots prepared from BSA-FA (A), BSA (B), BSA- AA (C) and BSA-FM (D).

XPS spectra for C-dots prepared from (A) BSA-FA and (B) the high resolution C1s spectra; from (C) BSA and (D) the high resolution C1s spectra; from (E) BSA-AA and (F) the high resolution C1s spectra and from (G) BSA-formaldehyde and (H) the high resolution C1s spectra.

One-photon cellular imaging of Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells under bright field (A) and 405nm excitation (B) with C-dots derived from BSA-FA. (C) Merged image of (A) and (B). Control cells under bright field (D) and 405nm excitation (E) without C-dots. (F) The merged image of (D) and (E). Scale bar=20μm.
Emission spectra of N-doped C-dots from BSA-FA by two-photon excitation (A) and a schematic illustration of two-photon FL mechanism in N-doped C-dots from BSA-FA (B).

Two-photon cell imaging under (A) bright field and (B) 750nm excitation with C-dots derived from BSA-formic acid. (C) The merged image of (A) and (B). Scale bars=20μm. (D) Quadratic relationship of the fluorescence (FL) intensity of C-dots from BSA-FA with the different excitation laser powers at 750nm (P: as measured at the focal plane).

Two-photon cellular nuclear imaging using TAT peptide modified C-dots (A), PI (B and E), C-dots (D). (C) Merged image of (A) and (B). (F) Merged image of (D) and (E). The excitation wavelength of carbon dots was 750nm and the capture range of wavelength was 380–560nm. The excitation wavelength of PI was 543nm, and the capture range of wavelength was 600–700nm. Scale bars=20μm.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: FA: formic acid; AA: acetic acid; FM: formaldehyde. N-content was calculated by measuring the XPS peak intensity ratio N.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots derived from bovine serum albumin and formic acid with one- and two-photon fluorescence for live cell nuclear imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Seafood Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, School of Food Science and Technology, Dalian Polytechnic University, 1 Qinggongyuan, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Seafood Science and Technology
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaoning Normal University, Dalian 116029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biotechnology, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 457 Zhongshan Road, Dalian 116023, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with both one- and two-photon fluorescence have drawn great attention for biomedical imaging. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots were facilely developed by one-pot hydrothermal method using bovine serum albumin and formic acid as carbon sources. They are highly water-soluble with strong fluorescence when excited with ultraviolet or near infrared light. The carbon dots have a diameter of ∼8.32nm and can emit strong two-photon induced fluorescence upon excitation at 750nm with a femtosecond laser. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer analysis revealed that the carbon dots contained three components, C, N and O, corresponding to the peak at 285, 398 and 532eV, respectively. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analysis revealed that there are carboxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface, which allowed further linking of functional molecules. pH stability study demonstrated that the carbon dots are able to be used in a wide range of pH values. The fluorescence mechanism is also discussed in this study. Importantly, these carbon dots are biocompatible and highly photostable, which can be directly applied for both one- and two-photon living cell imaging. After proper surface functionalization with TAT peptide, they can be used as fluorescent probes for live cell nuclear-targeted imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have drawn great attention because of their unique properties including good photo-stability, ultrasmall particle sizes, excitation tunable emission wavelength, low cytotoxicity, and high water-solubility [1,2], which possess great potential applications in small active molecules sensing, [3] light conversion agents [4], one-photon [5,6] and two-photon-induced fluorescence biological imaging [7]. Unlike semiconductor quantum dots containing toxic heavy metal, C-dots containing carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and hydrogen elements are hardly considered as toxic to the environment and are more biocompatible for their use in biomedical imaging [8]. Notably, two-photon-induced fluorescence imaging with C-dots possesses advantages such as minimized autofluorescence background, large penetration depth and reduced photodamage in biological samples. Therefore, it is highly desired to develop a biocompatible C-dot probe with both one- and two-photon-induced fluorescence for biomedical imaging.

Thanks to the recent development in nanomaterials science, many methods including arc-discharge, [9] laser ablation, [10] electrochemical oxidation, [11] hydrothermal strategy, [12] chemical oxidation, [13,14] and microwave-assisted pyrolysis [15,16] have been reported for the C-dot synthesis. All kinds of raw materials like candle soot [17], plant soot [5], small molecular chemicals [18] and orange juice [19] were used as carbon sources for the synthesis of C-dots. To further enhance the fluorescence (FL) performance, two strategies such as surface passivation and heteroatom doping have been explored for high quality C-dot synthesis. As for the former, polymers like polyethylene glycol (PEG) diamine, polyetherimide (PEI), poly-l-lysine (PLL), 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13-tridecanediamine (TTDDA) and chitosan were used for C-dot surface passivation and our works demonstrated the significant importance of surface modification in improving the fluorescence quantum yields [5], [8,20,21]. Sun et al. [10] reported the use of poly(propionylethylenimine-co-ethylenimine) (PPEI-EI) to modify C-dots with strong two-photon fluorescence for human breast cancer cells imaging. However, surface passivation still suffers from the drawbacks of complex reaction steps, tedious purification process, and functionalized groups occupying the reactive groups on the C-dot surface. Alternatively, heteroatom doping methods has attracted plenty of interesting for their applications in the improvement of PL performance of C-dots. For example, Chandra et al. [22] reported the synthesis of sulphur-doped C-dots with a fluorescence quantum yield of 11.8% without any additional surface passivation which have strong potential for use in bioimaging applications. Silicon-doped as well as phosphorus atom-doped C-dots with enhanced fluorescence have been developed for bioimaging and multimodal sensing applications [23,24]. Recently, the boron-doped C-dots based on the reaction of boric acid with urea have been reported, which exhibit efficient multi-color fluorescence under both single- and two-photon excitation [25]. Nevertheless, the facile synthesis of heteroatom-doped biocompatible C-dots with both one- and two-photon-induced fluorescence under femtosecond laser excitation remains a challenge for biological applications.

Recent research revealed that nitrogen (N)-doping strategy possesses great advantages in the improvement of quantum yields and N-doped C-dots have shown excellent properties in biological sample imaging and catalysis. Zhu et al. [26] reported a facile and green synthesis of N-doped C-dots via soy milk with favorable fluorescence and good electrocatalytic activity (quantum yield: 2.6%). Ma et al. [27] reported a one-pot ultrasonic synthesis of N-doped C-dots with a quantum yield of 6.65% using glucose and ammonium hydroxide as carbon sources. The prepared C-dots not only exhibited clear upconversion fluorescence properties, but also excellent photocatalytic property. Zhang and Chen [28] adopted folic acid to prepare N-doped C-dots (quantum yield: 15.7%) through a hydrothermal strategy as a “on-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions. Xu et al. [29] developed a general strategy to prepare N-doped C-dots by carbonization of 3-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-l-alanine (l-DOPA), l-histidine, and l-arginine as model precursors. All these N-doped C-dots with strong fluorescence have shown great potential as probes in bioimaging applications and provide an opportunity for more useful applications. Up to date, only a few reports were focused on the two-photon fluorescence property of the C-dots without complex surface passivation under femtosecond laser excitation, and their application for both one- and two-photon bioimaging [30].

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), a globular protein derived from cows, possesses nitrogen elements, a large number of amino and carboxyl groups, which may serve as a potential precursor for C-dot synthesis. Zhang et al. [12] has reported the one-photon fluorescence of C-dots derived from BSA in the presence of surface passivation reagent TTDDA. To the best of our knowledge, no effort has been focused on the synthesis of N-doped C-dots with both one- and two-photon fluorescence produced from BSA. Herein, we reported a facile one-step synthesis method for the preparation of N-doped C-dots through the hydrothermal reaction of BSA with formic acid (FA) without further surface passivation steps for both one- and two- photon-induced fluorescence bioimaging. The method is effective, labor-saving and no additional passivation steps are required. The good biocompatibility, excellent photostability and minimized autofluorescence pay the way for the potential application of the C-dots in biological imaging.

BSA was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Formic acid (FA, HCOOH, >88%) was purchased from Tianhe Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Acetic acid (AA, CH3COOH, >99.5%) was purchased from Guanghua Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Formaldehyde (FM, CH2O, 37–40%) and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Sephadex gel G-25 was obtained from GE Healthcare (Fairfield, San Diego, USA). Benzotriazol-1-yl-oxytripyrrolidinophosphonium, hexafluorophosphate (PyBOP), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole hydrate (HOBt), quinine sulphate, fluorescein, N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA), propidium iodide (PI) and methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl, >99.5%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, >96%) were purchased from Dalu Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). Phosphate (H3PO4, >85%) was purchased from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Boric acid (H3BO3, >99.5%) was purchased from Bodi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide (GRKKRRQRRRPQ) on beads (loading rate=0.56mmol/g) was purchased from Minghao Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells were purchased from Cell Bank of Type Culture Collection of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on an electron microscope (JEM-2100, JEOL), using a 200kV accelerating voltage. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer. FL spectra were recorded by fluorospectrophotometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Burker Vector 22 spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 500 to 4000cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific) were used to characterize the chemical composition using an Escalab 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. N-content was calculated by measuring the XPS peak intensity ratio N. Emission lifetime was measured on a Horiba Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer and using a 376nm laser as an excitation source. Zeta potential was determined by laser Doppler velocimetry at 25°C using a Nano ZS90 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, U.K).

BSA (0.9g, 1.35×10−5 mol) was added to 20mL of water, followed by adding 0.32g (88%) of FA (6.1×10−3 mol). The mixtures were heated hydrothermally in a stainless steel autoclave at a rate of 3°Cmin−1 and held at 200°C for 10h. The excess precursors and impurities were removed by filtration with a 0.22-μm membrane and the C-dots were further purified through a Sephadex G-25 Gel filtration desalting column. The first four fluorescent fractions (2mL per fraction) were collected and lyophilized. The obtained N-doped C-dots were stored at 4°C for further use. The C-dots from BSA-acetic acid and BSA-formaldehyde were prepared according to the above procedure by using the same molar ratio of organic reagents to BSA. The C-dots from BSA were prepared without adding any additional organic reagent.

The carbon dots (200mg) derived from BSA-FA, PyBop (32.3mg, 0.062mmol), HOBt (8.4mg, 0.062mmol) and DIPEA (0.1mL, 0.60mmol) were dissolved in 2mL of DMF and reacted with the TAT peptide (103mg, 0.058mmol) on beads at room temperature for 48h to conjugate the BSA-FA-dots at the N-terminal of the TAT peptide. After the reaction, the resin was completely washed with DMF, dichloromethane, water, and methanol three times each. The TAT-C-dot conjugate was then removed from the resin using trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) solution (TFA 86%, thioanisole 5%, 1,2-ethanedithiol 5%, phenol 2%, water 2%). The TAT-C-dot conjugate was isolated from 20mL of ether and then purified using a Sephadex G-25 gel chromatographic column.

Fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) was measured by using quinine sulfate in 0.10M H2SO4 solution as the standard. Absolute values are calculated according to the following equation: Φ x
=Φstd IxA std ηx 2 /I std Axη std 2. In the equation, Φ x is the quantum yield; Φstd refers to the quantum yield of reference fluorophore, quinine sulphate solution; Ix and I std are the fluorescence intensities of the C-dots and the standard, and Ax and A std are the optical densities of the C-dots and the standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 was chosen as a standard with a quantum yield Φstd =0.54 at 350nm. ηx and η std represent the refractive index of the solvents for the C-dots and the standard, respectively. The absorbencies of all the samples in 1.0cm cuvette were kept less than 0.1 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2×104 cells mL−1 and cultured for 24h in 10% serum containing MEM medium supplemented with penicillin (100 unitsmL−1) and streptomycin (100μgmL−1) in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37°C. After incubation with different concentrations (0, 0.04, 0.08, 0.16, 0.32, 0.63, 1.25, 2.5, 5.0mgmL−1, n =3) of C-dots for 24h, 20μL of MTT (5mgmL−1) reagent was added to each cell well. After further incubation for 4h, the cells were washed with PBS three times and 100μL of DMSO was added. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 570nm using a Microplate Reader (Wellscan MK3, Labsystems). The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation: cell viability [%]=(ODtreated/ODcontrol)×100% (ODcontrol is measured in the absence of agent, and ODtreated donates the intensity obtained in the presence of C-dots).

Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells were incubated with 1mgmL−1 of C-dots or TAT-C-dot conjugate aqueous solution in Φ 20mm cell culture dishes at 37°C. After 24h, the cells were washed in PBS buffer. Immediately, cells were imaged using an Olympus (Japan) laser-based point scanning FV1000 confocal fluorescent microscope. A 40× objective lens was used in xyz mode with 800×800 pixel resolution. A 405nm laser (26 %, 30% power) was used for excitation with one-photon emitted light collected between 425 and 475nm. Control cells without C-dots were imaged at the same parameter conditions. For cellular nuclear specific imaging, a 750nm laser (15% power) was used for excitation with two-photon emitted light collected between 380 and 560nm. The excitation wavelength of PI was 543nm, and the capture range of wavelength was 600–700nm.

The N-doped C-dots were prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal method using BSA/FA as precursors. In a typical synthesis, BSA (0.9g) was dissolved in water, followed by adding FA (0.32g). C-dots from BSA, BSA-AA and BSA-FM were also prepared as the control. The C-dots derived from BSA-formic acid were also surface modified with TAT peptide, via the amidation reaction between the amino group of the peptide and carboxylic group of the carbon dots, for cellular nuclear specific imaging.

The prepared N-doped C-dot aqueous solution displays a yellowish color with a long-term homogenous phase at room temperature. The formation and morphology of the C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The most nanoparticles are well dispersed on the grid surface (Fig. 1 A) and are distributed in the range from 6 to 11nm (Fig. 1B). The average size is about 8.32nm in diameter by the calculation of one hundred randomly-selected C-dots. HRTEM image inserted in Fig. 1A demonstrates that the as-prepared C-dots are nearly spherical and amorphous nanoparticles without noticeable lattice fringes, which is consistent with previous reported results [28]. Moreover, the C-dots aqueous solution exhibits a very intense blue color when the C-dots were irradiated with UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1B).

Fig. 2 A shows the UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra of the C-dots derived from BSA-FA. It can be seen that the C-dots display a broad peak at 275nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. This is similar to those of the control C-dots prepared from BSA, BSA-AA and BSA-FM shown in Fig. 2B–D. The broad peak at 275nm was ascribed to the n–π * transition as reported in previous work [31]. The optical absorption edge is observed to be 390nm, suggesting a 3.18eV optical bandgap of the N-doped C-dots. The absorbance spectra in the visible and near infrared region were measured and no obvious absorbance peak was found in the near infrared region. This is consistent with previous work [30]. As for the FL emission spectra (Fig. 2A), the C-dots showed the maximum emission peak at 407nm under the excitation wavelength of 320nm and the fluorescence intensity decayed with the increase of excitation wavelength. The control C-dots showed the maximum emission peaks at 420nm under the excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–320nm (Fig. 2B–D). Table 1 summarizes the physicochemical parameters of the C-dots. The strong FL of the N-doped C-dots may result from the emissive traps of the N-doping surface [10]. Unlike the control samples exhibiting an excitation-dependent FL behavior, the emission wavelength of the C-dots derived from BSA-FA is nearly independent of excitation wavelength (when the excitation wavelength was changed from 320 to 400nm, the emission wavelength showed almost no shift, Fig. 2A), which is also reported in previous work [32]. As we know, excitation-dependent FL behaviors of the C-dots reflect the effect from different particle sizes and a distribution of surface states. The analysis of TEM results reveal that the size of C-dots derived from BSA-FA is very uniform with a quite narrow size distribution (6–11nm). The surface states of the N-doped C-dots should be rather uniform. Therefore, such excitation independent FL characteristics might due to the similar N-doped surface states without affecting the band gap of the C-dots [28,33]. The zeta-potential analysis of the four types of C-dots reveal that the values are negative (Table 1), suggesting the existance of the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on their surface. By selecting quinine sulfate as the standard, the relative fluorescence quantum yield of the C-dots derived from BSA-FA was 17.1% (Table 1), which was the highest value among the four types of the C-dots. The supposed reason might be due to the increase of crosslinking of the FA and BSA through a series of reactions between aldehyde group, carboxyl group and amino groups of the BSA to form double bond between carbon and nitrogen (CN) during the hydrothermal reaction process. The resulting double bond between carbon and nitrogen (CN) in the C-dots nanostructure would have enhanced the fluorescence emission.

The elements analysis of XPS revealed that the C-dots contained three components, C, N and O, corresponding to the peak at 285, 398 and 532eV, respectively (Fig. 3 ). Since the FA, AA and FM have no nitrogen element, BSA is the only nitrogen source in this study. Although BSA contains cystine units, the quantity of sulfur (S) element was too small to be detected in the final products. The high-resolution scans of the C1s peak showed the carbons at least possessed three existence environments, corresponding to sp2 of CC at 284.5eV, sp3 of CO at 285.5eV and CN/CO at 287.5eV, respectively (Fig. 3B, D, F and H). The high resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. S1) demonstrate the presence of both pyridinic type (399.7eV) and pyrrolic type (400.6eV) N atoms [32]. This reveals that the C-dots may have hydroxyl, amide, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups. The total ratios of N to C for C-dots prepared from BSA-FA, BSA, BSA-AA, BSA-FM were ca. 0.21:1, 0.14:1, 0.13:1 and 0.14:1, respectively (Fig. 3A, C, E and G). The results clearly indicated that the C-dots derived from BSA-FA had more nitrogen element, which were much higher than those of N-doped C-dots synthesized by using CCl4 and NaNH2 as staring materials [18].

The C-dots were further characterized with FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2, before hydrothermal reaction, the FTIR spectrum of BSA showed characteristic absorption bands of amine, sp3 CH and amide at 3374, 2906, 1654 and 1535cm−1, respectively. After the formation of C-dots, the absorption band at 3374cm−1 shifted to 3400–3424cm−1 (Fig. S2b–e), indicating the generation of OH bond. As for the band at 1654cm−1 which changed to the range of 1665–1675cm−1 after the formation of C-dots were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN, revealing the formation of conjugated amide bonds. Moreover, the vibration bands at 1400–1413cm−1 demonstrated the presence of the respective aromatic amine (e.g., pyrrole ring). Unlike the control C-dots, the FTIR spectrum of C-dots derived from BSA-FA displayed a unique absorption band at 1600cm−1 (Fig. S2c), which ascribed the presence of CN bonds. All the results confirmed that the formation of such amide and CN double bonds for C-dots derived from BSA-FA due to the conjugation between the COOH or CHO groups of the FA and NH2 groups of BSA. In addition, the FTIR and XPS results reveal the existence of carboxyl and carboxylic groups, which are well consistent with the negative values of zeta-potential analysis of the four types of C-dots (Table 1). The available carboxyl and carboxylic groups of the C-dots were useful which allowed further linking of functional molecules by using the convenient N-hydroxysuccinimide chemistry.

Fluorescence lifetime is an intrinsic characteristic of C-dots indicating the period of the C-dots remains in its excited state prior to returning to its ground state. The parameters of the lifetime measurement were summarized in Table S1. The decay of the one-photon FL emission was non-monoexponential for the C-dots from BSA-FA as shown in Fig. S3. The average fluorescence lifetime of C-dots assessed by time-resolved FL measurements was 9.02±0.06 nanoseconds, which is comparable to those of the previous reports [15,16]. Such short lifetime of the FL emission demonstrates that the radiative recombination of the excitons gives the rise of fluorescence [34]. The non-monoexponential behavior of the lifetime suggests that different emissive sites are present on the C-dot center or surface traps [35].

Moreover, the C-dots derived from BSA-FA showed excellent photostability in water without any further surface modification as compared with the conventional organic dyes, fluorescein. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots remained unchanged even after being irritated with a laser for 220s, whereas that of fluorescein decreased 40% at the same period. The super photostability of the C-dots is of benefit for their potential applications as fluorescent probes in more harsh conditions. Another interesting phenomenon is that pH environment may affect the FL emission of the C-dots. Fig. S5 shows the pH effect on the intensity of the C-dots from BSA-FA. The emission intensity at 410nm decreased as the pH value increased from 2 to 11, but not significantly. This indicated that the radiative recombination of the energy-trapping sites on the C-dots is environment-sensitive with varying the pH value from acidic to alkaline. The reason of this phenomenon was possibly due to the C-dots prepared in an acidic environment during the deprotonation process, which may cause electrostatic doping/charging to the C-dots and shift their Fermi level [31,36].

It is essential for the C-dots to possess good biocompatibility for their potential applications as fluorescent probes in bio-medical fields. For this reason, the cytotoxicity of C-dots from BSA-FA was assessed after the Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells treated with different doses of C-dots by a MTT assay. After incubation, the cell viability was more than 77% even at C-dot concentration as high as 5mgmL−1 (Fig. S6). Thus the present C-dots derived from BSA and FA might be considered to be low toxic and biocompatible as a fluorescent probe for bio-imaging.

To demonstrate the capability of the prepared C-dots as fluorescent probes for live cell imaging, in vitro bioimaging study was carried out using Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells incubated with C-dots (BSA-FA C-dots with the highest quantum yield) without any further surface passivation by a confocal fluorescent microscopy. Fig. 4 A and B show the bright-field and one-photon confocal fluorescent images of the Tca-8113 cells incubated with 1mgmL−1 of C-dots in the medium for 24h, and then well washed for imaging. The bright-field image of the cells is clear visualized and the cancer cells incubated with the C-dots exhibit brightly blue color upon illuminating at 405nm. The majority of the fluorescent signal of C-dots appeared to come from the membrane cytoplasmic area of the tumor cells without invading the nucleus area as shown in Fig. 4C. However, much gloomier fluorescence can be observed in the control cells without incubation with C-dots at the same condition (Fig. 4D–F). These results suggest that these C-dots have potential in cellular imaging.

Moreover, the C-dots derived from BSA-FA were observed to possess clear multi-photon absorption ability in the near infrared range, which is expected to be useful for the two-photon cell imaging. Fig. 5 A shows the two-photon emission spectra of N-doped C-dots from BSA-FA by two-photon excitation wavelength of 710–870nm. The two-photon emission peak position shifted to longer wavelengths and the intensity gradually decreased with increased excitation wavelengths. The C-dots showed the maximum emission peak at 477nm under the excitation of 770nm femtosecond laser. The two-photon FL mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 5B. Similar to the property of N-doped graphene quantum dots, [5] the lone pair electrons from the N-doped C-dots would enlarge the π-conjugated system, [37] leading to the elevation of primary HOMO° orbit to a higher energy (HOMO) orbit. This would result in a decrease of the bandgap and redshift of the FL emission. When the as-prepared C-dots are excited with a femtosecond laser, the electrons absorb a pair of photons and are excited from HOMO orbit to LUMO orbit. After the excited electrons relaxing to its ground state (HOMO) undergoing a radiative route, a short wavelength FL emission with higher energy can be observed in the two-photon process.

To assess the capability of the N-doped C-dots for two-photon bioimaging, we carried out the in vitro cellular imaging by incubation the Tca-8113 tongue squamous cancer cells with N-doped C-dots. Fig. 6 A–C show the bright-field and two-photon fluorescent images of live cells incubated with C-dots, wherein we can see brightly green color upon excitation at 750nm with a femtosecond laser. In contrast, no visible fluorescence was detected in control cells without incubation with C-dots. Similar to the result of traditional one-photon fluorescent imaging, the visible signal mainly comes from the cytoplasm. Moreover, the two-photon fluorescence was investigated by excitation the C-dots sample with high photon density oscillator (λ center =750nm, 113MHz repetition rate, 70fs pulse width, OPAL BB, Spectra Physics) pumped by a femtosecond Ti:Sapphire laser (Tsunami, Spectra Physics). The quadratic relationship between the laser power and the FL intensity was displayed in Fig. 6D clear confirmed that the two-photon excitation was responsible for the FL emission observed in Fig. 6B.

Next, the N-doped C-dots derived from BSA-FA were further surface modified with TAT peptide to overcome the problem of the uniform distribution in the membrane cytoplasmic area of the tumor cells. This is more favorable for cellular nuclear specific imaging. The UV–vis absorption spectra were used to characterize the C-dots before and after surface modified with TAT peptide. As shown in Fig. S7, before conjugation with peptide, the C-dots exhibited an obvious absorption peak at 275nm and no peak was observed after conjugation with TAT peptide (TAT-C-dot conjugate), suggesting the formation of the TAT-C-dot conjugate. Fig. 7 shows the two-photon cellular nuclear specific imaging using TAT peptide modified C-dots. By using the PI as a nuclear tracker, the TAT-peptide functionalized C-dots were observed in the nuclear region by two-photon FL imaging (Fig. 7A and C), while there was no signal of the cellular nuclear for the C-dots without TAT peptide modification (Fig. 7D and F). The TAT peptide is derived from the trans-activator of transcription of human immunodeficiency virus and it could overcome the lipophilic barrier of the cellular membranes and deliver the carbon dots into the cellular nuclear. This result proved that the developed carbon dots can specifically target the cellular nuclear after proper surface functionalization, demonstrating that the prepared C-dots may have potential for high-contrast two-photon fluorescent cellular-specific bioimaging.

In conclusion, N-doped C-dots were facilely prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method using BSA and FA as raw materials for the first time. The C-dots exhibited strong one- and two-photon induced fluorescence, good photostability and excellent biocompatibility, which can be directly applied in both one- and two-photon fluorescence cellular imaging, as well as cell nuclear-targeted bioimaging. It is reasonable to believe that the doping of nitrogen can lead a new kind of surface state of the C-dots responsible for the observed enhanced FL [25,32]. Even though the exact mechanism is still unclear, it is expected that the N-doping two-photon-induced fluorescent carbon dots might have potential in more practical imaging applications [38].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (91227126, 81441052), National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project of China (2013YQ17046307), Nature Science Foundation of Liaoning Province, China (2013020177).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2015.09.008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
